title: Why Seasons Aren't About Distance
script_summary: Explaining how Earth's seasons are determined by axial tilt, not distance from the Sun.

segments.voiceover: Did you know Earth is closest to the Sun in January?
So why are we freezing in winter?
It's all about axial tilt—not distance. Earth's axis is tilted at 23.5 degrees.
This tilt causes different parts of Earth to receive varying sunlight throughout the year.
In summer, the Northern Hemisphere tilts toward the Sun, heating things up!
So, the next time you enjoy the sun, remember—it’s not about how close we are.
Follow for more mind-blowing science facts!

full_voiceover: Did you know Earth is closest to the Sun in January? So why are we freezing in winter? It's all about axial tilt—not distance. Earth's axis is tilted at 23.5 degrees. This tilt causes different parts of Earth to receive varying sunlight throughout the year. In summer, the Northern Hemisphere tilts toward the Sun, heating things up! So, the next time you enjoy the sun, remember—it’s not about how close we are. Follow for more mind-blowing science facts!
